title: Quick Start: Complete Reproducible Data Workflow
text: Create report uses data workflow (raw data → processing → functions → visualization). Create analysis/report/report.Rmd:
code: --- title: "Palmer Penguins Bill Dimensions Analysis" author: "Reproducible Research Team" date: "2026-06-29" output:   html_document:     toc: true     code_folding: show ---    ## Introduction  Analysis of bill length and bill depth measurements across three penguin species from the Palmer Station Antarctica LTER.  This report demonstrates the complete data workflow: - Raw data → Processing → Analysis → Visualization  ## Data Processing   ``` r library(dplyr) library(ggplot2)  # Load the processed data (function from R/data_utils.R) penguins <- load_penguin_data()  # Preview data head(penguins) ```  ## Summary Statistics   ``` r # Calculate summary by species (function from R/data_utils.R) summary_stats <- summarize_by_species(penguins) knitr::kable(summary_stats, digits = 2, caption = "Bill dimensions by species") ```  ## Visualization  ### Bill Dimensions by Species   ``` r # Generate plot using function from R/bill_analysis.R bill_plot <- create_bill_plot(penguins) print(bill_plot)  # Save plot dir.create(here::here("analysis/figures"), showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE) ggplot2::ggsave(here::here("analysis/figures/bill_scatter.png"),                 bill_plot, width = 8, height = 6) ```  ## Summary  The analysis reveals distinct bill dimension patterns across penguin species: - Data processed from raw measurements (Step 3) - Functions in R/ provide reusable analysis components - Unit tests ensure correctness

text: Auto-Snapshot Architecture exit R (inside container), zzcollab automatically: Runs renv::snapshot() capture package dependencies renv.lock Reports success reminds commit changes need manually run renv::snapshot() — just exit R commit updated renv.lock. Auto-restore startup intentionally disabled (ZZCOLLAB_AUTO_RESTORE=false Dockerfile) baked library authoritative container re-resolve packages network every startup. add packages install.packages() inside container, exit R (auto-snapshot runs), rebuild image (make docker-build), new packages baked .

Neither command touches data, code, analysis. Run upgrading zzcollab adopting compendium created earlier version.

title: Quick Start: Complete Reproducible Data Workflow
text: R version: Specifies exact R version (4.4.0) System dependencies: system libraries (libcurl, libssl, etc.) Base image: Foundation consistent environment Environment variables: Locale system settings
code: # Generated by zzcollab -- do not edit manually. FROM rocker/tidyverse:4.4.2@sha256:<digest>   # pinned by zzc at init time  # OCI provenance labels LABEL zzcollab.template.version="2.4.0" \       zzcollab.r.version="4.4.2" \       zzcollab.ppm.snapshot="2026-06-01"  # Library lives outside the project bind-mount so the baked environment # is not shadowed when the host working tree is mounted at runtime. ENV LANG=en_US.UTF-8 LC_ALL=en_US.UTF-8 TZ=UTC \     RENV_PATHS_LIBRARY=/opt/renv/library \     RENV_PATHS_CACHE=/opt/renv/cache \     RENV_CONFIG_REPOS_OVERRIDE="https://packagemanager.posit.co/cran/__linux__/noble/2026-06-01" \     ZZCOLLAB_CONTAINER=true \     ZZCOLLAB_AUTO_RESTORE=false  RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \     libcurl4-openssl-dev libssl-dev libxml2-dev && \     rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*  RUN R -e "install.packages('renv')" RUN mkdir -p /opt/renv/library /opt/renv/cache COPY renv.lock renv.lock RUN R -e "renv::init(bare=TRUE, force=TRUE, restart=FALSE); renv::restore()"

title: Quick Start: Complete Reproducible Data Workflow
text: Critical R options: Version controlled copied Docker Pinned settings: stringsAsFactors, contrasts, na.action, digits, OutDec RNG discipline: RNGkind() pinned ensure seed reproducibility across R versions Important: zzcollab’s .Rprofile template pins RNG kind critical session options. settings version-controlled copied Docker, ensuring consistent behaviour across team members CI/CD runs.
code: # .Rprofile (version controlled, generated by zzcollab)  # Pin RNG algorithm so stochastic analyses are reproducible across R versions. # R 3.6.0 changed the default sample.kind, silently breaking seeded analyses. RNGkind("Mersenne-Twister", "Inversion", "Rejection")  # Reproducibility options — applied on both host and in container options(   stringsAsFactors = FALSE,   contrasts = c("contr.treatment", "contr.poly"),   na.action = "na.omit",   digits = 7,   OutDec = "." ) # renv activation and auto-snapshot-on-exit are handled automatically. # Do not add source("renv/activate.R") manually; it is in the template.

previous_headings: What You Built > The Five Pillars of Reproducibility
title: Quick Start: Complete Reproducible Data Workflow
text: Environment variables affect computational results silently critical reproducibility: Locale settings (LANG, LC_ALL): Control string sorting order (e.g., “Åland” sorts differently US vs Swedish locales) Affect number parsing (comma vs period decimal separator) Impact factor level ordering statistical models Timezone (TZ): Affects date-time arithmetic aggregation Eliminates daylight saving time complications Ensures consistent temporal analyses across team members Parallel processing (OMP_NUM_THREADS): Controls thread count deterministic computation Single-threaded (=1) ensures reproducible results Multi-threaded can produce different results run due floating-point order Real-world impact: Two researchers running identical code different locales timezones can produce different results without explicit environment variable specification.
code: # Environment variables set in Dockerfile ENV LANG=en_US.UTF-8        # Standardize locale ENV LC_ALL=en_US.UTF-8      # Override all locale categories ENV TZ=UTC                  # No daylight saving time ENV OMP_NUM_THREADS=1       # Deterministic single-threaded execution

previous_headings: What You Built > The Five Pillars of Reproducibility
title: Quick Start: Complete Reproducible Data Workflow
text: Key Insight: Dockerfile = Environment foundation (R version, system libraries, environment variables) renv.lock = Exact packages (source truth R dependencies) .Rprofile = R session settings (critical options affect behavior) Source Code = Analytical logic (actually runs, including random seeds) Research Data = Empirical observations (analyses operate ) five pillars version controlled Git, representing necessary sufficient components complete reproducibility. Without one pillar, independent reproduction analysis becomes impossible.
code: # Someone reproduces your analysis 5 years from now: git clone https://github.com/rgt47/penguin-bills.git cd penguin-bills  # 1. Dockerfile builds environment (R version + system deps) docker build -t penguin-env . docker run -it -v $(pwd):/project penguin-env  # 2. renv.lock installs exact package versions renv::restore()  # 3. Source code runs the analysis source("analysis/scripts/01_process_data.R") rmarkdown::render("analysis/report/report.Rmd")  # Result: Identical output to your original analysis

Pillar 1 - Dockerfile: R version explicitly specified System dependencies documented Environment variables set (LANG, LC_ALL, TZ, OMP_NUM_THREADS) Pillar 2 - renv.lock: packages contributors included Exact versions repository sources Committed version control Pillar 3 - .Rprofile: Critical options version controlled copied Docker RNGkind pinned seed reproducibility across R versions renv activation auto-snapshot managed template automatically Pillar 4 - Source Code: Random seeds explicit stochastic operations Analysis scripts, functions, tests committed Clear computational workflow documented Pillar 5 - Research Data: Raw data preserved (read-, original files) Data documentation complete (data dictionary, provenance) Processing scripts link raw derived data Data quality notes documented

title: Quickstart: Collaborative Analysis Workflow
text: guide walks complete collaborative analysis workflow using zzcollab. Two developers collaborate analysis mtcars dataset, demonstrating proper data management, testing, reporting, version control practices. Key terms vignette. terms guide leans ; reintroduced first use. Full definitions vignette('glossary'). Compendium: project folder, holding data, code, write-together whole thing can re-run unit. Archetype: project type pick creation (analysis, manuscript, package, simulation, blog); shapes starter layout. guide uses analysis. Profile: Docker image bundle environment built (minimal, analysis, rstudio). Distinct archetype: profile image, archetype layout. Feature: independently switchable capability (package backend, Docker, CI, data hashing, code-quality hooks, unit tests, cloud launch) toggled zzc toggle zzc add/zzc rm. Capture vs validation: capture features (backend Docker) pin environment set capture level; validation features (tests, CI, code quality) check . Capture level (L0 L3): locked-project , ‘just findable’ (L0) ‘rebuilt verified’ (L3). Team image: Docker image team lead builds renv.lock packages baked , collaborators skip install wait. Image digest: manifest checksum (.team-image-digest) lets every member pull byte-identical team image. Workflow Summary: Developer 1 sets workspace, prepares data, writes initial analysis Developer 1 pushes GitHub Developer 2 clones, adds visualization, pushes changes Developer 1 pulls changes finalizes report Prerequisites: Git installed configured Docker installed running GitHub account SSH keys configured zzcollab repository cloned

Building multiple regression models",     "interactively. Biometrics, 37, 391-411."   ),   "analysis/data/raw_data/README.md" )

title: Quickstart: Collaborative Analysis Workflow
text: Create script reads raw CSV extracts first half analysis. Create analysis/scripts/01_prepare_data.R:
code: # Exit R first with q(), then create the script file on host # 01_prepare_data.R # Purpose: Extract first half of mtcars and add derived variables  library(dplyr)  prepare_mtcars_data <- function(input_path, output_path) {   raw_data <- read.csv(input_path, row.names = 1)    n_rows <- nrow(raw_data)   subset_data <- raw_data[1:(n_rows %/% 2), ]    processed_data <- subset_data |>     mutate(       efficiency_class = case_when(         mpg >= 20 ~ "high",         mpg >= 15 ~ "medium",         TRUE ~ "low"       ),       weight_kg = wt * 453.592,       power_to_weight = hp / wt     )    saveRDS(processed_data, output_path)   processed_data }  if (sys.nframe() == 0) {   prepare_mtcars_data(     input_path = "analysis/data/raw_data/mtcars.csv",     output_path = "analysis/data/derived_data/mtcars_processed.rds"   ) }

title: Quickstart: Collaborative Analysis Workflow
text: Move core function package’s R/ directory testing reuse. Create R/data_functions.R:
code: #' Prepare mtcars data for analysis #' #' @param data A data frame with mtcars structure #' @return A data frame with additional computed columns #' @export add_derived_variables <- function(data) {   if (!all(c("mpg", "wt", "hp") %in% names(data))) {     stop("Input must contain mpg, wt, and hp columns")   }    data |>     dplyr::mutate(       efficiency_class = dplyr::case_when(         mpg >= 20 ~ "high",         mpg >= 15 ~ "medium",         TRUE ~ "low"       ),       weight_kg = wt * 453.592,       power_to_weight = hp / wt     ) }  #' Validate mtcars data structure #' #' @param data A data frame to validate #' @return TRUE if valid, throws error otherwise #' @export validate_mtcars_data <- function(data) {   required_cols <- c("mpg", "cyl", "disp", "hp", "drat", "wt",                      "qsec", "vs", "am", "gear", "carb")    missing_cols <- setdiff(required_cols, names(data))   if (length(missing_cols) > 0) {     stop("Missing required columns: ", paste(missing_cols, collapse = ", "))   }    if (nrow(data) == 0) {     stop("Data frame is empty")   }    if (any(data$mpg <= 0, na.rm = TRUE)) {     stop("mpg must be positive")   }    if (any(data$hp <= 0, na.rm = TRUE)) {     stop("hp must be positive")   }    TRUE }

title: Quickstart: Collaborative Analysis Workflow
text: ZZCOLLAB projects use tinytest. Test files live inst/tinytest/ contain bare top-level expectations (test_that() wrapper). Create inst/tinytest/test-data-validation.R:
code: # test-data-validation.R # Tests for data integrity and validation functions  raw_data_path <- 'analysis/data/raw_data/mtcars.csv'  # Raw data file exists and is readable expect_true(file.exists(raw_data_path)) data <- read.csv(raw_data_path, row.names = 1) expect_inherits(data, 'data.frame')  # Expected dimensions expect_equal(nrow(data), 32L) expect_equal(ncol(data), 11L)  # Required columns present required_cols <- c('mpg', 'cyl', 'disp', 'hp', 'drat', 'wt',                    'qsec', 'vs', 'am', 'gear', 'carb') expect_true(all(required_cols %in% names(data)))  # Values within expected ranges expect_true(all(data$mpg > 0 & data$mpg < 50)) expect_true(all(data$cyl %in% c(4, 6, 8))) expect_true(all(data$hp > 0 & data$hp < 500)) expect_true(all(data$wt > 0))  # Derived data has the correct subset size (only if it has been created) derived_path <- 'analysis/data/derived_data/mtcars_processed.rds' if (file.exists(derived_path)) {   expect_equal(nrow(readRDS(derived_path)), 16L) }

title: Quickstart: Collaborative Analysis Workflow
text: Create inst/tinytest/test-data-functions.R:
code: # test-data-functions.R # Tests for R/data_functions.R  # validate_mtcars_data accepts valid data valid_data <- data.frame(   mpg = c(21, 22), cyl = c(6, 4), disp = c(160, 140),   hp = c(110, 93), drat = c(3.9, 3.85), wt = c(2.6, 2.3),   qsec = c(16.5, 18.6), vs = c(0, 1), am = c(1, 1),   gear = c(4, 4), carb = c(4, 2) ) expect_true(validate_mtcars_data(valid_data))  # validate_mtcars_data rejects missing columns expect_error(validate_mtcars_data(data.frame(mpg = 21, cyl = 6)),              pattern = 'Missing required columns')  # validate_mtcars_data rejects empty data expect_error(validate_mtcars_data(mtcars[0, ]), pattern = 'empty')  # validate_mtcars_data rejects invalid mpg bad_data <- mtcars[1:2, ] bad_data$mpg[1] <- -5 expect_error(validate_mtcars_data(bad_data),              pattern = 'mpg must be positive')  # add_derived_variables creates the expected columns result <- add_derived_variables(mtcars[1:5, ]) expect_true('efficiency_class' %in% names(result)) expect_true('weight_kg' %in% names(result)) expect_true('power_to_weight' %in% names(result))  # add_derived_variables calculates efficiency_class correctly ec_data <- data.frame(   mpg = c(25, 17, 12),   wt = c(2.5, 3.0, 3.5),   hp = c(100, 150, 200) ) expect_equal(add_derived_variables(ec_data)$efficiency_class,              c('high', 'medium', 'low'))  # add_derived_variables calculates weight_kg correctly kg_result <- add_derived_variables(data.frame(mpg = 20, wt = 1, hp = 100)) expect_equal(kg_result$weight_kg, 453.592, tolerance = 0.001)  # add_derived_variables requires the correct input columns expect_error(add_derived_variables(data.frame(x = 1, y = 2)),              pattern = 'must contain mpg, wt, and hp')

title: Quickstart: Collaborative Analysis Workflow
text: Edit analysis/report/report.Rmd:
code: --- title: "Fuel Efficiency Analysis: mtcars Subset" author: "Developer 1" date: "2026-06-29" output:   html_document:     toc: true     toc_float: true     theme: flatly bibliography: references.bib ---  ```{r report-setup, include=FALSE} knitr::opts_chunk$set(   echo = TRUE,   message = FALSE,   warning = FALSE,   fig.width = 8,   fig.height = 5 )  # Load package functions from R/ directory devtools::load_all(here::here()) ```  ## Introduction  This analysis examines fuel efficiency patterns in a subset of the Motor Trend Car Road Tests dataset. The raw data contains all 32 observations; the derived dataset extracts the first 16 observations for analysis.  ## Data Loading   ``` r library(dplyr) library(ggplot2) library(knitr)  # Load processed data data <- readRDS(here::here("analysis/data/derived_data/mtcars_processed.rds"))  # Display summary kable(head(data), caption = "First observations of processed data") ```  ## Summary Statistics   ``` r summary_stats <- data |>   group_by(efficiency_class) |>   summarise(     n = n(),     mean_mpg = mean(mpg),     mean_hp = mean(hp),     mean_weight_kg = mean(weight_kg),     .groups = "drop"   )  kable(summary_stats,       digits = 2,       caption = "Summary statistics by efficiency class") ```  ## Analysis Results  ### Fuel Efficiency Distribution   ``` r ggplot(data, aes(x = mpg, fill = efficiency_class)) +   geom_histogram(bins = 8, alpha = 0.7, color = "white") +   labs(     title = "Distribution of Fuel Efficiency",     x = "Miles Per Gallon",     y = "Count",     fill = "Efficiency Class"   ) +   theme_minimal() ```  <!-- Developer 2 will add additional visualizations here -->  ## Conclusions  *To be completed after collaborative additions.*  ## References

title: Quickstart: Collaborative Analysis Workflow
text: make docker-push-team builds multi-architecture image (linux/amd64 linux/arm64), pushes Docker Hub, writes manifest digest .team-image-digest. Committing file lets team members run make docker-pull-team get byte-identical image. zzc dockerhub simpler alternative pushes single-architecture image without recording digest.
code: make docker-build  # Multi-arch push (amd64 + arm64) and records digest to .team-image-digest: make docker-push-team  # Commit the digest so team members can pull the exact same image: git add .team-image-digest git commit -m "Record team image digest" git push

title: Quickstart: Collaborative Analysis Workflow
text: Developer 2 clones project loads team Docker image: zzc update minor maintenance command. checks project’s framework-managed template files (Makefile, CI workflows, similar) installed zzcollab version, refreshes stale, back-fills state record. Running right clone keeps collaborator’s framework files consistent team lead’s, without touching analysis code primary feature files. Confirm result zzc status (read-) zzc verify (coherence checks).
code: git clone git@github.com:myteam/mtcars-analysis.git cd mtcars-analysis  # Bring framework-managed template files in line with the installed # zzcollab version, and back-fill local state. zzc update  # Preferred: pull the exact digest the team lead recorded (bit-identical) make docker-pull-team          # reads .team-image-digest committed by team lead  # Alternative: build from Dockerfile (always works, takes 5-15 min) make docker-build

code: zzc status                     # read-only: shows features and capture level zzc verify                     # coherence checks (files agree with state) make r # Verify data is present readRDS("analysis/data/derived_data/mtcars_processed.rds") |> head()  # Run existing tests to ensure environment is correct tinytest::run_test_dir('inst/tinytest')  q()

title: Quickstart: Collaborative Analysis Workflow
text: Developer 2 adds new plotting function. Create R/plot_functions.R:
code: #' Create power-to-weight scatter plot #' #' @param data Processed mtcars data frame #' @param show_labels Logical, whether to show car labels #' @return A ggplot object #' @export plot_power_weight <- function(data, show_labels = TRUE) {   if (!"power_to_weight" %in% names(data)) {     stop("Data must contain power_to_weight column")   }    p <- ggplot2::ggplot(     data,     ggplot2::aes(x = weight_kg, y = hp, color = efficiency_class)   ) +     ggplot2::geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +     ggplot2::labs(       title = "Horsepower vs Weight by Efficiency Class",       x = "Weight (kg)",       y = "Horsepower",       color = "Efficiency"     ) +     ggplot2::theme_minimal() +     ggplot2::theme(legend.position = "bottom")    if (show_labels) {     p <- p + ggplot2::geom_text(       ggplot2::aes(label = rownames(data)),       hjust = -0.1,       vjust = 0.5,       size = 3     )   }    p }  #' Create cylinder comparison boxplot #' #' @param data Processed mtcars data frame #' @return A ggplot object #' @export plot_mpg_by_cylinder <- function(data) {   ggplot2::ggplot(data, ggplot2::aes(x = factor(cyl), y = mpg)) +     ggplot2::geom_boxplot(fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.7) +     ggplot2::geom_jitter(width = 0.2, alpha = 0.5) +     ggplot2::labs(       title = "Fuel Efficiency by Number of Cylinders",       x = "Number of Cylinders",       y = "Miles Per Gallon"     ) +     ggplot2::theme_minimal() }

title: Quickstart: Collaborative Analysis Workflow
text: Edit analysis/report/report.Rmd include new visualizations. Add following sections existing histogram: Update Conclusions section:
code: ### Power vs Weight Analysis   ``` r # Functions loaded via devtools::load_all() in setup chunk plot_power_weight(data, show_labels = TRUE) ```  This scatter plot reveals the relationship between engine power and vehicle weight. Higher efficiency vehicles (green) cluster in the lower-left region, indicating lower weight and moderate horsepower.  ### Cylinder Comparison   ``` r plot_mpg_by_cylinder(data) ```  The boxplot demonstrates the expected inverse relationship between cylinder count and fuel efficiency. Four-cylinder vehicles show the highest MPG values, while eight-cylinder vehicles demonstrate lower fuel efficiency. ## Conclusions  This analysis of the mtcars subset reveals several key findings:  1. **Efficiency Classification**: The dataset contains a mix of low, medium,    and high efficiency vehicles based on the 15 and 20 MPG thresholds.  2. **Weight-Power Relationship**: There is a clear positive correlation    between vehicle weight and horsepower, with efficiency class serving as    a useful categorization.  3. **Cylinder Effect**: Vehicles with fewer cylinders consistently achieve    better fuel economy, supporting the expected engineering trade-off between    power and efficiency.  ## Session Information   ``` r sessionInfo() ```

Push multi-arch image and record digest (preferred) make docker-push-team            # writes .team-image-digest; commit this file  # Alternative: single-arch push without digest recording zzc dockerhub                    # Pushes to dockerhub-account/project:latest zzc dockerhub --tag v1.0         # Or with an explicit version tag # Check if renv.lock changed since last push git log --oneline -3 -- renv.lock  # If changed, rebuild and push make docker-build && zzc dockerhub

title: Quickstart: Reproducibility Feature Toggles
text: Every project can combination . None required start. Package backend (renv / Nix / none): records exact package versions installs repeatable. Docker environment: runs project container identical setup across machines. CI workflows: re-run checks (render report) automatically every push. Data-integrity hashing: checksum file (data-manifest.sha256) detects raw data file changes. Code-quality hooks: pre-commit checks (styler lintr) tidy lint code commit. Unit tests: tinytest test scaffold. Cloud launch: .devcontainer project opens cloud editor Binder.

title: Quickstart: Reproducibility Feature Toggles

Cancelling changes nothing. opens current state adapts interactive tools installed, preferring richest one available: fzf presents two-pane selector, gum tickable list, neither plain prompt per feature. three converge confirmation step. Selecting fzf. backend appears first single-select list live information panel re-renders move cursor, trade-choice visible decide: Use arrow keys move, Enter choose, Esc cancel wizard. features appear checklist, panel now describing highlighted feature: Press Tab Space tick highlighted feature ; cursor holds place box updates. Enter applies selection, Esc cancels. Selecting gum plain prompts. Without fzf, installed gum renders checklist single tickable list. neither tool, wizard asks one question time: Whichever interface used, wizard previews difference applies confirmation; applying, new state printed: wizard also catches common mismatches : Turning CI report offers scaffold one, since render workflow needs something render. Choosing renv Docker notes renv alone reaches level L1, Docker reaches L2 (levels explained ). Choosing Nix backend turns Docker default, Nix already pins environment without container.
code: zzc toggle Current: backend=renv  docker=on  ci=on  data=off  code-quality=off  tests=on  cloud=on > renv          Backend: renv (R lockfile)   nix           Pins:    L1 - exact R package versions   none          Image:   pinned only when Docker is on                 Deps:    renv, bundled in the project > [x] docker          Docker environment   [ ] ci              Adds:  Dockerfile + make r / make rstudio   [ ] data            Pins:  L2 - the full environment, for sharing   [x] tests           Cost:  image build time and disk   [ ] cloud Backend [renv/nix/none] (default: renv): renv   Docker environment        [on/off] (current: on): on   CI workflows              [on/off] (current: on): on   Data integrity hashing    [on/off] (current: off): on   Code quality (pre-commit) [on/off] (current: off): on   Unit testing (tinytest)   [on/off] (current: on): on   Cloud launch (devcontainer) [on/off] (current: on): on Planned changes:   - data: off -> on   - code-quality: off -> on  Apply these changes? Capture versus validation. Capture features pin computational environment set reproducibility level; validation features check result. facts behind zzc status come generated .zzcollab-state record (base image, digest, install mode, archetype), status never re-parse Dockerfile.

what: Choosing a package backend: renv, Nix, or none
title: Quickstart: Reproducibility Feature Toggles
text: backend single choice, checklist item. Switching backends removes old file writing new one, renv.lock flake.nix never coexist. Dockerfile present, switching backend regenerates Dockerfile matching install mode (restore renv.lock, install DESCRIPTION via pak), reusing base image R version remembered .zzcollab-state, question re-asked. Nix backend wires way: make r enters nix develop, host R targets run via nix develop -c, CI installs Nix runs nix develop.
code: zzc renv                 # renv.lock (the default backend) zzc rm renv              # drop to a DESCRIPTION-only project (L0/L1)  zzc nix                  # flake.nix pinned to a nixpkgs revision (L2) zzc rm nix

what: Choosing a container runtime: Docker, Podman, or Apptainer
title: Quickstart: Reproducibility Feature Toggles
text: runtime engine runs image; set configuration Makefile reads (overridable per call): Multi-architecture team publishing uses Docker buildx stays Docker-specific; local build run honor configured runtime.
code: zzc config set docker-runtime podman      # daemonless, drop-in for `make r` make r                                     # runs via podman  zzc config set docker-runtime apptainer   # HPC: exec a SIF make sif                                   # build env.sif from the image make CONTAINER_RUNTIME=apptainer r         # apptainer shell env.sif

title: Quickstart: Reproducibility Feature Toggles
text: status shows level declared, verify confirms . coherence tier needs build: checks files agree .zzcollab-state (backend conflict, install mode matches renv.lock presence, base image matches, R versions agree, data matches data-manifest.sha256). reproduction tier rebuilds image runs test suite inside . earns L3; success stamps verified .zzcollab-state, later regeneration drops stamp changed environment reverts unverified automatically.
code: zzc verify VERIFY  /path/to/penguins-study  Coherence   [ ok ] backend unambiguous (renv)   [ ok ] state record present (.zzcollab-state)   [ ok ] install mode (renv) consistent with renv.lock presence   [ ok ] Dockerfile copies renv.lock and renv.lock exists   [ ok ] Dockerfile base image matches state (rocker/tidyverse)   [ ok ] renv.lock is valid JSON with a Packages section   [ ok ] R version consistent across sources (4.6.0)   [ ok ] raw data matches data-manifest.sha256  verify: coherence passed (0 warnings, 1 skipped)   Run 'zzc verify --full' to rebuild and reach L3 (verified). zzc verify --full

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Reproducibility computational research exists spectrum. guide presents progressive, five-level approach reproducibility using ZZCOLLAB, allowing researchers adopt tools incrementally based project needs collaboration requirements. level adds specific guarantees reproducibility introducing additional complexity. Understanding tradeoffs enables informed decisions appropriate reproducibility investments different research contexts. Key terms vignette. New vocabulary? terms guide uses; defined first use. Full definitions vignette('glossary'). Computational reproducibility: getting numerical results data code. renv: R package giving project isolated, version-pinned package library. Lockfile (renv.lock): JSON record every package version needed rebuild environment. Version pinning: recording exact dependency version drift machines time. Docker / container / image / Dockerfile: tool packages software environment; running instance, template, recipe builds . Unit test / testthat / tinytest: focused check one piece code behaves intended, frameworks writing . Continuous integration (CI): automatically building testing code every push. Code rot / environmental drift: gradual breakage working code time dependencies change underneath .

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Level 1: Basic R Project - Manual package management Level 2: renv - Automated dependency tracking Level 3: renv + Docker - Complete environment isolation Level 4: renv + Docker + Unit Testing - Computational correctness Level 5: renv + Docker + Unit Testing + CI/CD - Automated validation

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: standard R project structure manual package management. Packages installed user’s global R library using install.packages().

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: my-project/ ├── .Rproj ├── R/ │   └── functions.R ├── data/ │   └── raw_data/ ├── scripts/ │   └── 01_analysis.R └── README.md

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Simple: additional tools concepts learn Fast setup: Start coding immediately Flexible: Easy experiment new packages Low overhead: configuration files maintain Standard workflow: Familiar R users

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: version tracking: Package versions recorded Update fragility: update.packages() can break existing code Collaboration friction: “Works machine” problems common Time-dependent failure: Code may stop working months later due package updates Implicit dependencies: record packages actually required System-dependent: Results may differ across operating systems

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Appropriate : Personal exploratory analysis Short-lived projects (< 1 week) Learning R packages Quick prototypes intended sharing Analysis revisit appropriate : Published research Team collaboration Long-term projects Code ’ll share others

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: # January 2024: Code works library(dplyr) data %>% select(x, y) %>% filter(x > 0)  # dplyr 1.1.0  # June 2024: After update.packages() # dplyr 1.2.0 introduces breaking change # Code produces different results or fails

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: renv package creates project-specific R libraries records exact package versions lockfile. project isolated dependencies independent global R library.

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: my-project/ ├── .Rproj ├── renv/                    # Project-specific package library ├── renv.lock               # Exact package versions (auto-generated) ├── .Rprofile               # Auto-activates renv (auto-generated) ├── R/ ├── data/ ├── scripts/ └── README.md

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: # Initialize renv in existing project install.packages("renv")  # One-time global install renv::init()  # Or create new project with ZZCOLLAB mkdir my-project && cd my-project zzc minimal                      # Minimal profile includes renv

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: # Install packages (now project-specific) # Both methods work - install.packages() is simpler in ZZCOLLAB Docker containers install.packages("dplyr") install.packages("ggplot2") # For GitHub packages: remotes::install_github("user/package")  # Work normally library(dplyr) library(ggplot2)  # Save dependency state # In ZZCOLLAB Docker containers: snapshot happens automatically on exit! # Outside Docker: run renv::snapshot() manually  # Collaborator restores exact versions renv::restore()   # Installs from renv.lock

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Version locking: Exact package versions recorded renv.lock Project isolation: Projects interfere Collaboration support: Team members get identical package versions Explicit dependencies: renv.lock documents required packages Time resilience: Project works months/years later packages Safe updates: Test updates isolation committing Minimal learning curve: Familiar R workflow install.packages()

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Additional storage: project stores package copies (~100-500MB typical) Initial setup time: First renv::restore() downloads packages Snapshot discipline: Must remember renv::snapshot() package changes (automated ZZCOLLAB Docker containers - see Level 3) R version dependency: Still depends user’s R version System library dependency: Still depends system libraries (gdal, geos, etc.) Platform differences: Windows vs Mac vs Linux can still cause issues

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Appropriate : Research projects intended publication Team collaboration (2+ researchers) Long-term projects (> 1 month) Code shared publicly Reproducible analysis workflows Still insufficient : Cross-platform guarantees (Windows/Mac/Linux differences) System library dependencies (geospatial, databases) Complete environment isolation Exact R version requirements

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Guaranteed: R package versions across users/time guaranteed: R version, system libraries, OS

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: # Day 1: Start project renv::init() install.packages("tidyverse") renv::snapshot()  # Day 30: Add new analysis install.packages("lme4") # ... write code ... renv::snapshot()  # Remember to snapshot!  # Note: With ZZCOLLAB Docker (Level 3), snapshot happens automatically on container exit!  # Year 2: Collaborator joins git clone project # In R: renv::restore()  # Gets exact package versions from Day 30

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Docker containers provide complete environment isolation including R version, system libraries, operating system. Everything runs identical Linux environment regardless host operating system.

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: my-project/ ├── .Rproj ├── renv/ ├── renv.lock ├── Dockerfile              # Environment definition ├── docker-compose.yml      # Container orchestration ├── .dockerignore           # Files to exclude from container ├── Makefile                # Convenient Docker commands ├── R/ ├── data/ ├── scripts/ └── README.md

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: # Create project with Docker environment mkdir my-project && cd my-project zzc minimal                      # Full setup (init + renv + docker)  # Or add Docker to existing renv project # (requires manual Dockerfile creation)

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: # Start development environment make r  # Now inside container (identical Linux environment) R > install.packages("tidyverse")  # Standard R command works! > # ... work normally ... > quit()  # Exit container - AUTO-SNAPSHOT HAPPENS AUTOMATICALLY! exit  # Auto-snapshot architecture (October 2025) # - renv::snapshot() runs automatically on container exit # - No manual snapshot command required! # - renv.lock automatically updated and validated (via zzrenvcheck)  # Collaborator gets identical environment git clone project make r  # Builds identical container

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Complete isolation: R version, packages, system libraries controlled Cross-platform consistency: environment Windows/Mac/Linux System dependency management: GDAL, GEOS, database drivers included Reproducible system state: Dockerfile version-controls entire environment Development/production parity: environment locally servers Easy collaboration: “Pull run” - local R installation needed Multiple R versions: Different projects can use different R versions

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Storage overhead: Docker images 800MB-3GB per project Learning curve: Docker concepts (images, containers, volumes) Initial setup time: Building Docker image (2-20 minutes depending mode) Resource usage: Docker daemon must run background File permission complexity: User IDs can cause permission issues Slower package installation: First install container takes longer Platform limitations: ARM64 (Apple Silicon) vs AMD64 compatibility issues

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Appropriate : Cross-platform collaboration (Windows/Mac/Linux teams) Projects system dependencies (geospatial, databases) Research requiring exact R version specification Complex environment dependencies Production deployment analysis pipelines Long-term archival (10+ years) Overkill : Solo projects single platform Simple analysis dependencies Short-term exploratory work

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Guaranteed: R version, packages, system libraries, OS guaranteed: Code correctness, analytical validity, absence bugs

what: The Critical Gap: Environment vs Correctness
title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Docker ensures everyone runs code identical environment, ensure code produces correct results. reproducibility paradox: Level 3 guarantees consistency correctness. Level 4 addresses gap.
code: # Everyone gets the same WRONG answer in Docker calculate_mean <- function(x) {   sum(x)  # BUG: forgot to divide by length }  # Reproducible? Yes - same environment, same (wrong) result # Correct? No - analytical error

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
code: # Team lead creates environment with a geospatial base image mkdir climate-study && cd climate-study zzc analysis                     # Full setup; ML packages added via renv zzcollab docker --base-image rocker/geospatial  # Add geospatial base image zzc github                       # Create private GitHub repo  # Environment provides: # - R version: latest stable # - System libraries: gdal, geos, proj # - Base packages: tidyverse, sf, terra, raster  # Build and share team image (installs packages from renv.lock) make docker-build make docker-push-team            # Optional but efficient for team  # Team member joins git clone https://github.com/mylab/climate-study cd climate-study make docker-build               # Build from project Dockerfile make r  # Inside container - everyone has same environment R --version  # Same R version for all sf::sf_extSoftVersion()  # Same GDAL version for all

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: ZZCOLLAB provides predefined Docker profiles balance image size capabilities:
code: # Minimal: Essential R development (~650MB) zzc minimal  # Analysis: Data analysis with tidyverse (~1.2GB) [RECOMMENDED] # Statistical and machine learning packages are added via renv zzc analysis  # RStudio Server in the browser zzc rstudio  # Manuscript and report rendering (LaTeX/Quarto via the rocker/verse base image) zzcollab docker --base-image rocker/verse  # Specialized domains (e.g. GIS, genomics) use a custom base image: zzcollab docker --base-image rocker/geospatial  # List all available profiles zzc help profiles

code: # Without tests: Silent error propagates analyze_data <- function(data) {   # BUG: Uses sd() instead of var() for variance   variance <- sd(data$x)  # WRONG    # Analysis appears to work but results are incorrect   result <- mean(data$x) / variance   return(result) }  # With tests: error caught immediately # This expectation fails, exposing the bug x <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5) expect_equal(calculate_variance(x), var(x))  # FAILS

what: When Testing Is Not Worth It
title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Testing costs. Skip testing : One-time exploratory scripts reused Simple visualizations calculations Wrapper functions call tested functions Interactive analysis share Focus testing effort : Data processing pipelines complex logic Statistical calculations beyond simple base R functions Functions used across multiple analyses Code shared published

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Scenario: Student analyzing dataset thesis. share code advisor. May extend analysis publication. Duration: 3-6 months Recommended Level: Level 2 Rationale: Need ensure advisor can reproduce results. Long enough packages might update analysis.
code: # Initialize renv renv::init() install.packages("tidyverse") install.packages("lme4")  # Analysis code...  # Snapshot before sharing with advisor renv::snapshot()  # Advisor runs: renv::restore()  # Gets exact package versions

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Scenario: Three universities collaborating climate analysis. Different platforms (Windows, Mac, Linux). Complex geospatial dependencies. Custom spatial interpolation algorithms. Duration: 2 years Recommended Level: Level 4 Rationale: Cross-platform team requires Docker. Complex custom algorithms require unit tests ensure correctness. Geospatial packages (sf, terra) complex system dependencies.
code: # Team lead setup with a geospatial base image mkdir precipitation && cd precipitation zzc analysis                     # Full setup; ML packages added via renv zzcollab docker --base-image rocker/geospatial  # Add geospatial base image zzc github                       # Create private GitHub repo  # Build and share team image (installs packages from renv.lock) make docker-build make docker-push-team            # Optional but efficient for team  # Add tests under inst/tinytest/ (tinytest scaffolding already present): # - Tests for spatial interpolation algorithms # - Tests for data quality validation make docker-test  # Team members (any platform) git clone https://github.com/climate-team/precipitation cd precipitation make docker-build               # Build from project Dockerfile make r  # Identical environment on all platforms  # Inside container - everyone has same GDAL, GEOS versions # Tests validate spatial algorithms work correctly

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Scenario: Developing R package CRAN submission. Contributors around world. Must pass R CMD check Windows/Mac/Linux. Duration: Ongoing Recommended Level: Level 5 Rationale: Multiple contributors require automated testing. CRAN submission requires validation multiple platforms. CI/CD ensures every contribution passes checks merge.
code: # Package maintainer setup mkdir mypackage && cd mypackage zzc analysis                     # Full setup (init + renv + docker) zzc github                       # Create private GitHub repo with CI/CD  # Testing and CI/CD included automatically # Every contributor push triggers: # - R CMD check on Ubuntu # - Test suite execution (unit tests) # - Documentation validation # - Dependency checking  # Pull requests show CI status # Maintainer only merges if CI passes

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Scenario: Automated weekly report generation database. Runs server. Code rarely changes must always work. Report includes statistical summaries data quality checks. Duration: Ongoing Recommended Level: Level 4 Rationale: Production deployment requires Docker. Statistical calculations data quality checks tested prevent silent errors automated reports. CI/CD (Level 5) optional code changes infrequently.
code: # Setup with Docker for production (minimal profile sufficient for reporting) mkdir weekly-report && cd weekly-report zzc minimal                      # Full setup with minimal profile  # Add tests for report calculations under inst/tinytest/: # - Statistical summaries (means, medians, etc.) # - Data quality checks (missing data, outliers) # - Report formatting functions make docker-test  # Deploy to server docker build -t weekly-report . docker run -v /data:/data weekly-report Rscript generate_report.R  # Optional: Add CI/CD (Level 5) if code complexity grows or team expands zzc github                       # Creates GitHub repo with CI/CD workflows

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: renv creates project-specific library : Installing packages renv/library/ instead global library Recording versions renv.lock (JSON format) Activating via .Rprofile sets .libPaths()
code: # Before renv::init() .libPaths() # [1] "/usr/local/lib/R/site-library"  # Global library  # After renv::init() .libPaths() # [1] "/project/renv/library/R-4.3/x86_64-pc-linux-gnu"  # Project library # [2] "/usr/local/lib/R/site-library"                    # Fallback

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Docker creates isolated environment : Base image (rocker/r-ver:4.3.1) - specific R version System libraries (GDAL, GEOS, etc.) installed via apt-get R packages installed via Dockerfile instructions Project files mounted volume Container runs isolated process filesystem, project directory mounted live editing.
code: FROM rocker/r-ver:4.3.1 RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y libgdal-dev RUN R -e "install.packages('renv')" WORKDIR /project

code: # .github/workflows/r-package.yml jobs:   check:     runs-on: ubuntu-latest     container: rocker/tidyverse:latest     steps:       - uses: actions/checkout@v4       - name: Validate dependencies (zzrenvcheck)         run: make check-renv

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Forgetting renv::snapshot() (Note: Automated ZZCOLLAB Docker!): Package installation methods (work ZZCOLLAB Docker containers): Note: ZZCOLLAB Docker containers, install.packages() works correctly packages automatically captured renv.lock exit container.
code: # Wrong workflow (manual renv without Docker) install.packages("new-package") # ... use package in code ... git commit  # renv.lock not updated!  # Collaborator runs: renv::restore()  # Doesn't get new-package # Code fails with "package not found"  # Correct workflow (manual renv) install.packages("new-package") renv::snapshot()  # Update renv.lock git add renv.lock git commit  # ZZCOLLAB Docker auto-snapshot (October 2025): # With ZZCOLLAB Docker containers, snapshot happens automatically on exit! make r install.packages("new-package")  # Standard R command works in Docker exit  # Auto-snapshot runs automatically - no manual command needed! git add renv.lock && git commit # Standard R command (recommended in ZZCOLLAB Docker containers) install.packages("dplyr")  # Auto-captured in renv.lock on exit  # For GitHub packages install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("user/package")

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: File permission issues: Forgetting exit container git operations: Editing files outside container: Files edited host (Mac/Windows) visible container, editors create temp files can cause issues. Best practice: edit host, run container.
code: # Inside container (running as root) touch output.csv  # Outside container (your user) rm output.csv  # Permission denied!  # Solution: Container should run as non-root user (zzcollab does this) # Wrong make r git commit  # Runs inside container  # Right make r # ... work ... exit  # Exit container first git commit  # Run on host

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: testing edge cases: Testing implementation details instead behavior: Forgetting run tests committing:
code: # Inadequate test: only checks the return type result <- prepare_data(penguins) expect_true(is.data.frame(result))  # Better test includes edge cases expect_equal(nrow(prepare_data(penguins)), 333L)       # Normal case expect_equal(nrow(prepare_data(penguins[0, ])), 0L)    # Empty data all_na <- penguins all_na$body_mass_g <- NA expect_equal(nrow(prepare_data(all_na)), 0L)           # All missing # Wrong: tests how the function is implemented (breaks under refactoring) expect_true(grepl('filter', deparse(body(prepare_data))))  # Right: tests what the function does (works regardless of implementation) result <- prepare_data(penguins) expect_false(anyNA(result$body_mass_g)) # Wrong workflow git commit -m "Update analysis" make docker-test  # Fails - should have tested first  # Right workflow make docker-test  # Test first git commit -m "Update analysis"

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: testing locally pushing: Ignoring CI failures: CI failures must fixed immediately. Ignoring leads : - Broken main branch - Inability merge pull requests - Loss confidence tests
code: # Wrong workflow git commit -m "Fix analysis" git push  # CI fails, must fix and push again  # Right workflow (make check-renv runs zzrenvcheck) make check-renv make docker-test git commit -m "Fix analysis" git push  # CI passes

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Reproducibility binary - exists spectrum. ZZCOLLAB provides five levels increasing reproducibility guarantees: Level 1 (Manual): Quick start, guarantees Level 2 (renv): Package version guarantees Level 3 (renv + Docker): Complete environment guarantees Level 4 (renv + Docker + Unit Testing): Computational correctness guarantees Level 5 (renv + Docker + Unit Testing + CI/CD): Automated validation guarantees

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: level addresses different dimension: Environment Reproducibility (Levels 2-3): packages, R version, system libraries Computational Correctness (Level 4): Code produces analytically sound results Automated Verification (Level 5): Continuous validation everything still works

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Choose level appropriate project’s: Duration: Longer projects need reproducibility Team size: Larger teams need validation Platform diversity: Mixed platforms require Docker Analytical complexity: Complex calculations require tests Publication requirements: Published research needs high reproducibility Maintenance needs: Long-term projects need automated checks

title: Layered Approach to Reproducibility
text: Start simple migrate incrementally needs evolve: Week 1: Level 1 exploration Month 1: Add Level 2 (renv) committing project Month 2: Add Level 3 (Docker) collaborating cross-platform Month 3: Add Level 4 (Testing) analysis complexity grows Month 6: Add Level 5 (CI/CD) team expands goal reproducibility without unnecessary complexity. level adds value, also adds overhead. Match reproducibility investment project requirements.

title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: vignette explains create reusable team Docker images reduce project initialization time. Rather building new Docker image rocker base images project (15-20 minutes comprehensive builds), can: Build : Create comprehensive team image preferred packages Push Docker Hub: Store image central repository Reuse everywhere: New projects use team image base (~30 seconds setup) approach particularly valuable : Research labs multiple ongoing projects Teams requiring consistent package ecosystems Solo researchers working across multiple analyses Organizations standardized R environments Key terms vignette. New vocabulary? terms guide uses; defined first use. Full definitions vignette('glossary'). Team image: pre-built Docker image shared via registry every collaborator works byte-identical environment. Base image: starting Docker image project builds (e.g. rocker/rstudio, rocker/verse). Profile: Docker image bundle environment built , minimal, analysis, rstudio. Image digest pinning: referencing image immutable content hash (sha256:...) every pull byte-identical. .team-image-digest: committed file recording team image’s digest members pull exact image. Dockerfile: recipe builds project’s Docker image. renv.lock: JSON record every package version needed rebuild environment. make target: named Makefile command (e.g. make docker-build, make docker-push-team) wrapping container operation.

title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
code: # Project 1: Build from rocker/rstudio (15-20 minutes) mkdir study1 && cd study1 zzc analysis                     # Full setup (init + renv + docker) zzc github                       # Create private GitHub repo make docker-build make docker-push-team cd ..  # Project 2: Build from rocker/rstudio again (15-20 minutes) mkdir study2 && cd study2 zzc analysis zzc github make docker-build make docker-push-team cd ..  # Project 3: Build from rocker/rstudio again (15-20 minutes) mkdir study3 && cd study3 zzc analysis zzc github make docker-build make docker-push-team  # Total time for 3 projects: 45-60 minutes

title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
code: # One-time: Build analysis team base image (15-20 minutes) mkdir baseimage && cd baseimage zzc analysis                     # Full setup with analysis profile zzc github                       # Create private GitHub repo make docker-build make docker-push-team            # Push to Docker Hub for team access git add . git commit -m "Create team base image with comprehensive packages" git push cd ..  # Project 1: Build from shared Dockerfile (~5 minutes first time) mkdir study1 && cd study1 make docker-build               # Builds from project Dockerfile cd ..  # Project 2: Build from shared Dockerfile (faster with Docker cache) mkdir study2 && cd study2 make docker-build cd ..  # Project 3: Build from shared Dockerfile (faster with Docker cache) mkdir study3 && cd study3 make docker-build

title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: project rebuilds rocker base images, reinstalling packages.

text: New projects use pre-built team image base, avoiding package reinstallation.
code: One-Time Setup:   rocker/rstudio:latest → Install 47 packages → Team Base Image   (Docker Hub)            (15-20 minutes)       (mylab/baseimageecore-rstudio:latest)                                                  ↓ (push to Docker Hub)  Subsequent Projects:   mylab/baseimageecore-rstudio:latest → Project-Specific Setup → Project Image   (Docker Hub, pre-built)                (~30 seconds)            (mylab/study1core-rstudio:latest)

what: Step 1: Create Comprehensive Team Base Image
title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: team lead creates one-time base image preferred packages. : - Creates project structure DESCRIPTION, Makefile, etc. - Builds rocker/rstudio:latest - Installs analysis profile packages (renv, devtools, tidyverse, ) - Creates mylab/baseimage:latest - Pushes Docker Hub team access Profile options: Specialized domain images:
code: # Create analysis team base image # This takes 15-20 minutes but only needs to be done once mkdir baseimage && cd baseimage zzc analysis                     # Full setup with analysis profile zzc github                       # Create private GitHub repo make docker-build                # Installs packages from renv.lock make docker-push-team            # Push to Docker Hub for team git add . git commit -m "Create comprehensive team base image" git push cd .. # Analysis profile (recommended for general team bases) mkdir baseimage && cd baseimage zzc analysis                     # Full setup zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team git add . && git commit -m "Create analysis team base image" && git push cd ..  # Machine learning focused mkdir modeling-base && cd modeling-base zzc analysis                     # Full setup; ML packages added via renv zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team git add . && git commit -m "Create modeling team base image" && git push cd ..  # Minimal profile (lightweight) mkdir minimal-base && cd minimal-base zzc minimal                      # Full setup with minimal profile zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team git add . && git commit -m "Create minimal team base image" && git push cd .. # Bioinformatics (Bioconductor genomics) mkdir bio-base && cd bio-base zzcollab docker --base-image bioconductor/bioconductor_docker  # Bioconductor base zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team git add . && git commit -m "Create bioinformatics team base image" && git push cd .. # Add genomics packages via renv (install.packages / BiocManager) as needed  # Geospatial (GIS and spatial analysis) mkdir geo-base && cd geo-base zzcollab docker --base-image rocker/geospatial  # Geospatial base zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team git add . && git commit -m "Create geospatial team base image" && git push cd .. # Add spatial packages via renv (install.packages) as needed

what: Step 2: Verify Image on Docker Hub
title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: build completes, verify image available:
code: # Check image exists locally docker images | grep mylab/baseimage  # Verify on Docker Hub (requires login) docker manifest inspect mylab/baseimage:latest  # Or visit Docker Hub in browser # https://hub.docker.com/r/mylab/baseimage

what: Step 3: Use Team Base Image for New Projects
title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: Now team member can create new projects using team base image: : - Pulls mylab/study1:latest Docker Hub (available) - Creates project structure needed - Uses team base image foundation - Takes ~1-3 minutes instead 15-20 minutes Note: DockerHub optional efficient. team image available, building Dockerfile produces identical environment.
code: # Clone an existing project repository git clone https://github.com/mylab/study1.git cd study1  # Build Docker image from project Dockerfile make docker-build  # Start development make r

what: Example 1: Solo Researcher with Multiple Projects
title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: Dr. Smith works various analyses wants consistent environments.
code: # One-time setup: Create personal base image (Sunday afternoon) mkdir mybase && cd mybase zzc analysis                     # Full setup with analysis profile zzc github                       # Create private GitHub repo make docker-build                # Installs packages from renv.lock make docker-push-team            # Push to Docker Hub for reuse git add . && git commit -m "Create personal base image" && git push cd .. # Takes 15-20 minutes, grab coffee  # Monday: Start microbiome analysis mkdir microbiome-study && cd microbiome-study zzc analysis                     # Full setup zzc github # Ready in seconds (framework files created)  # Wednesday: Start climate modeling project mkdir climate-model && cd climate-model zzc analysis zzc github # Ready in seconds  # Friday: Start survey analysis mkdir survey-analysis && cd survey-analysis zzc analysis zzc github # Ready in seconds  # Total time: ~16 minutes vs 45-60 minutes (traditional approach)

what: Example 2: Research Lab with Team Collaboration
title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: genomics lab 5 researchers multiple ongoing studies. Team Lead (one-time setup): Team Members (joining existing projects):
code: # Create lab base image with bioinformatics tools mkdir labbase && cd labbase zzcollab docker --base-image bioconductor/bioconductor_docker  # Bioconductor base zzc github                       # Create private GitHub repo make docker-build                # Installs packages from renv.lock make docker-push-team            # Push to Docker Hub for team git add . && git commit -m "Create lab base image" && git push cd ..  # The Bioconductor base image provides genomics tooling # Additional packages can be added via install.packages() / BiocManager as needed # Researcher 1: Join existing cancer genomics project git clone https://github.com/genomicslab/cancer-study.git cd cancer-study make docker-build               # Build from project Dockerfile make docker-rstudio              # Start RStudio at localhost:8787  # Researcher 2: Join existing RNA-seq analysis git clone https://github.com/genomicslab/rnaseq-analysis.git cd rnaseq-analysis make docker-build make docker-rstudio  # Researcher 3: Create new variant calling project mkdir variant-calling && cd variant-calling zzcollab docker --base-image bioconductor/bioconductor_docker  # Bioconductor base zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team make docker-rstudio  # All 3 researchers ready in ~90 seconds total # Traditional approach: 45-60 minutes total

title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: data science consulting firm requires consistent package versions across client projects. DevOps Team (quarterly base image updates): Data Scientists (creating client projects):
code: # Q1 2025: Create standardized base image mkdir q1-2025-base && cd q1-2025-base zzc analysis                     # Full setup with analysis profile zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team git add . && git commit -m "Create Q1 2025 standardized base image" && git push cd ..  # Push to private Docker registry (optional) docker tag datasci-firm/q1-2025-base:latest \   registry.company.com/datasci-firm/q1-2025-base:latest docker push registry.company.com/datasci-firm/q1-2025-base:latest # Client A: Retail analytics mkdir client-a-retail && cd client-a-retail zzc analysis                     # Full setup zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team  # Client B: Financial modeling mkdir client-b-finance && cd client-b-finance zzc analysis zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team  # Client C: Healthcare dashboards mkdir client-c-health && cd client-c-health zzc analysis zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team  # All projects use identical Q1 2025 package versions # No "works on my machine" issues

title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: Create different base images different research domains:
code: # Bioinformatics base (Bioconductor packages) mkdir bio-base && cd bio-base zzcollab docker --base-image bioconductor/bioconductor_docker  # Bioconductor base zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team git add . && git commit -m "Create bioinformatics base image" && git push cd .. # Use for: genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics projects  # Geospatial base (sf, terra, raster) mkdir geo-base && cd geo-base zzcollab docker --base-image rocker/geospatial  # Geospatial base zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team git add . && git commit -m "Create geospatial base image" && git push cd .. # Use for: climate modeling, ecology, urban planning projects  # Machine learning base (ML packages) mkdir ml-base && cd ml-base zzc analysis                     # Full setup; ML packages added via renv zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team git add . && git commit -m "Create machine learning base image" && git push cd .. # Use for: predictive modeling, classification, regression projects  # Publishing base (LaTeX, Quarto, rmarkdown) mkdir pub-base && cd pub-base zzcollab docker --base-image rocker/verse   # LaTeX/Quarto via the rocker/verse base image zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team git add . && git commit -m "Create publishing base image" && git push cd .. # Use for: manuscripts, reports, reproducible documents

title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: Maintain version history reproducibility:
code: # Create dated base image mkdir base-2025-01 && cd base-2025-01 zzc analysis                     # Full setup with analysis profile zzc github make docker-build && make docker-push-team git add . && git commit -m "Create January 2025 versioned base image" && git push cd ..  # Tag with semantic version docker tag mylab/base-2025-01:latest \   mylab/base-2025-01:v1.0.0 docker push mylab/base-2025-01:v1.0.0  # Projects can reference specific versions # Modify project Dockerfile to use: # FROM mylab/base-2025-01:v1.0.0

what: Pattern 3: Incremental Updates to Base Images
title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: Update base images without rebuilding scratch:
code: # Add new package to existing base image docker run --rm -it mylab/baseimage:latest R  # Inside container: install.packages("newpackage") quit()  # Commit changes to new version docker commit <container-id> mylab/baseimage:v1.1.0 docker push mylab/baseimage:v1.1.0  # Or rebuild base image with updated packages cd baseimage make docker-build make docker-push-team # Leverages Docker layer caching for faster rebuilds

what: Environment Variables in Team Base Images
title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: zzcollab-generated Docker images automatically include reproducibility-critical environment variables. creating reusable team base images, verify present: Matters Team Images: team uses shared base image, team members inherit environment variable settings. ensures: Consistent string sorting across analyses Identical date-time calculations regardless team member location Reproducible parallel processing behavior locale-dependent result differences Verification: manually modify team base image Dockerfiles, ensure variables remain set. Removing can cause silent reproducibility failures across team.
code: # Reproducibility-critical environment variables (automatically included) ENV LANG=en_US.UTF-8        # Standardize locale ENV LC_ALL=en_US.UTF-8      # Override all locale categories ENV TZ=UTC                  # Eliminate daylight saving complications ENV OMP_NUM_THREADS=1       # Deterministic single-threaded execution # Check environment variables in team base image docker run --rm mylab/baseimage:latest env | grep -E "LANG|TZ|OMP"  # Expected output: # LANG=en_US.UTF-8 # LC_ALL=en_US.UTF-8 # TZ=UTC # OMP_NUM_THREADS=1

code: # Good: Indicates purpose and currency mylab/bioinformatics-base-2025 mylab/datascience-comprehensive mylab/shiny-apps-standard  # Avoid: Generic names mylab/base mylab/image1 mylab/test

title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere

The analysis profile provides the tidyverse # only; packages such as tidymodels or shiny are not pre-installed and must # be added via renv (renv::install(), then snapshot and rebuild the image). # In RStudio: library(tidyverse)  # If tests pass, promote to production docker tag mylab/baseimage-test:latest \   mylab/baseimage:latest docker push mylab/baseimage:latest

what: Should You Create a Reusable Team Base Image?
title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: Strong Yes : - work 3+ projects similar package requirements - team consistent development environment needs - frequently start new projects (monthly ) - Build time bottleneck workflow - need reproducible environments across projects Maybe : - 1-2 ongoing projects - Package requirements vary widely projects - rarely start new projects (quarterly less) - ’re experimenting different package ecosystems Probably : - Single project, infrequent updates - Extremely specialized package requirements per project - Storage constraints (Docker Hub free tier, limited disk space) - Team members different R version requirements

title: Reusable Team Images: Build Once, Use Everywhere
text: Reusable team base images provide substantial time savings teams solo researchers working across multiple projects: Key Benefits: - 73% time savings: ~30 seconds vs 15-20 minutes per project - Consistency: Identical package versions across projects - Collaboration: Team members share exact environments - Cost-effective: Free Docker Hub public repositories Recommended Workflow: 1. Create comprehensive team base image (one-time, 15-20 minutes) 2. Push Docker Hub team access 3. Use base image new projects (~30 seconds ) 4. Update base image quarterly needed Best : - Research labs multiple ongoing projects - Solo researchers diverse analyses - Organizations requiring standardized environments - Teams needing reproducible collaboration tools approach transforms zzcollab per-project build system reusable infrastructure platform, improving developer productivity maintaining reproducibility guarantees.

title: Reproducibility Infrastructure and the Replication Crisis: Positioning zzcollab in Light of the SCORE Project
text: SCORE project (Systematizing Confidence Open Research Evidence), seven-year DARPA-funded initiative involving 865 researchers, produced comprehensive empirical account scientific reproducibility date. nine papers, published Nature early 2026, document failure rates across three distinct dimensions reproducibility – computational, robustness, replication – assess capacity artificial intelligence predict studies hold . document synthesizes SCORE findings alongside four antecedent empirical studies provide mechanistic detail failure modes: Stodden et al. (2018) journal policy effectiveness, Silberzahn et al. (2018) multi-analyst variability, Camerer et al. (2018) replication high-profile social science experiments, Hardwicke et al. (2018) mandatory open data policy single journal. Together sources map precise causal account reproducibility failure identify architectural choices zzcollab address failure modes. Key terms vignette. New vocabulary? terms guide uses; defined first use. Full definitions vignette('glossary'). Computational reproducibility: getting numerical results data code. Reproducibility crisis: widespread finding many published results independently reproduced. Five Pillars (reproducibility): five artifacts treated jointly sufficient: Dockerfile, renv.lock, .Rprofile, source code, research data. Docker / container / image / Dockerfile: tool packages software environment; running instance, template, recipe builds . renv: R package giving project isolated, version-pinned package library. Continuous integration (CI): automatically building testing code every push. Data provenance / lineage: record data came transformed. Seed (set.seed) / RNG kind: fixed starting point making random results reproducible; generator whose default changed across R versions.

what: 1. Background: The Scale and Structure of the Problem
title: Reproducibility Infrastructure and the Replication Crisis: Positioning zzcollab in Light of the SCORE Project
text: replication crisis new phenomenon, empirical documentation become substantially precise past decade. Open Science Collaboration (2015) replicated 100 psychology papers matched original results 39 percent time. Camerer et al. (2016) replicated 18 laboratory economics experiments found 61 percent success rate, effect sizes averaging 66 percent originals. projects established scale problem. SCORE’s contribution diagnostic: rather asking whether study replicates, decomposes failure space three distinct empirical questions. Computational reproducibility: Given data code, new analyst obtain numerical results? Robustness: Given data different analytical methods, multiple independent teams reach qualitative conclusion? Replicability: Given new sample research question, original effect hold? separable failure modes distinct causes distinct remedies. study can computationally reproducible robust analytical choice; can replicate qualitatively producing substantially attenuated effect sizes. four antecedent papers reviewed address one dimensions controlled empirical designs, together provide mechanistic detail justifies specific infrastructure choices.

what: 2.1 Computational Reproducibility: Policy Is Necessary but Insufficient
title: Reproducibility Infrastructure and the Replication Crisis: Positioning zzcollab in Light of the SCORE Project
text: Stodden et al. (2018) provide direct empirical test whether journal policies produce computational reproducibility. sampled 204 computational papers published Science journal implemented mandatory data code sharing policy February 2011. Contacting corresponding authors underlying artifacts, found 44 percent emailed authors provided materials, yielding overall artifact recovery rate 44 percent (95% CI [0.36, 0.50]). 56 papers judged potentially reproducible materials, attempted replication random sample 22 succeeded 1. estimated replication rate full sample 26 percent (95% CI [0.20, 0.32]). Several qualitative findings study directly relevant zzcollab’s design. First, 13 percent papers mentioned hardware environmental settings – precise gap Dockerfile addresses. Second, code received authors typically modified since used generate published results, producing silent divergence archived analytical code. Third, Stodden et al. (2018) use term ‘research compendium’ refer bundle publication, data, code – structure zzcollab scaffolds default project layout. Fourth, conclusion policy requiring artifact remission upon request ‘improvement policy, currently insufficient reproducibility’; recommend instead journals verify deposit artifacts condition publication. Hardwicke et al. (2018) conducted complementary natural experiment, examining introduction mandatory open data policy journal Cognition 1 March 2015. policy substantially increased data availability: data available statements rose 25 percent pre-policy (104/417) 78 percent post-policy (136/174). availability usability. data nominally available, 62 percent -principle reusable (accessible, complete, understandable). team actually attempted reproduce key outcomes 35 articles -principle reusable data, achieved initial reproduction rate 31 percent without author assistance. author assistance, 63 percent eventually reproducible; 37 percent remained fully reproducible even author help. Two specific findings Hardwicke et al. (2018) deserve emphasis. First, note ‘authors may always aware hidden defaults employed statistical software. example, R defaults Welch t-test SPSS reports Student t-test Welch t-test’ (p. 14). marginal concern: 53 percent discrete reproducibility issues identified attributable incomplete ambiguous specification analysis procedure – hidden default choices original analyst never thought document. Second, paper written using R Markdown Code Ocean container capture software environment, directly demonstrating technology solve problem already exists. description experience reproducing underdocumented analysis – ‘assembling flat pack furniture without instruction booklet’ – captures practical cost absent infrastructure. SCORE’s computational reproducibility arm adds picture larger scale: re-running original code original data 143 papers, approximately 9 percent produced completely different results another 14 percent approximately . team reconstruct code independently, exact reproduction fell 50 percent. SCORE figures consistent Hardwicke et al. (2018)’s 31 percent unaided rate: absent specified environment analysis script, baseline computational failure rate across fields roughly 50 70 percent.

what: 2.2 Robustness: The Analytical Degrees of Freedom Problem
title: Reproducibility Infrastructure and the Replication Crisis: Positioning zzcollab in Light of the SCORE Project
text: Silberzahn et al. (2018) provide clearest experimental evidence SCORE’s robustness arm measures scale. Twenty-nine teams involving 61 analysts independently addressed research question – whether soccer referees likely give red cards dark-skinned players – using dataset 146,028 player-referee dyads. Analytic approaches varied widely: 29 teams used 21 unique combinations covariates model specifications ranging ordinary least squares regression zero-inflated Poisson regression hierarchical Bayesian models. Effect sizes ranged 0.89 2.93 odds-ratio units (Mdn = 1.31). Twenty teams (69%) found statistically significant positive effect; 9 teams (31%) . key finding variability results failure observable factor explain . Analysts’ prior beliefs hypothesis predict results. Expertise level – measured academic rank, graduate teaching, methodological publications – predict effect size significance. Peer ratings analysis quality showed association reported effect sizes. authors conclude: ‘uncertainty interpreting research results therefore just function statistical power use questionable research practices; also function many reasonable decisions researchers must make order conduct research’ [p. 354; Silberzahn et al. (2018)]. Silberzahn et al. (2018) explicit problem distinct p-hacking: ‘analysts incentive try different specifications choose one supported hypothesis…even , variability analytic choices led variability observed results’ (p. 352). source divergence set defensible undeclared choices made preprocessing model specification – covariate selection, handling non-independence across observations, distributional assumptions, exclusion criteria. choices made silently, governed partly software defaults partly field conventions encoded published artifact. SCORE’s robustness arm scales finding 100 papers across multiple social science disciplines: 57 percent papers yielded roughly result across least five independent analyst teams; one-third yielded precisely result.

title: Reproducibility Infrastructure and the Replication Crisis: Positioning zzcollab in Light of the SCORE Project
text: Camerer et al. (2018) – Social Sciences Replication Project (SSRP) – replicated 21 experimental studies published Nature Science 2010 2015. replications pre-registered Open Science Framework reviewed original authors data collection. Sample sizes average five times larger original studies. Thirteen 21 studies (62%) showed statistically significant effect direction original. effect size findings consequential understanding infrastructure can remedy. mean standardized effect size replications 0.249, compared 0.460 original studies – mean relative effect size 46.2 percent. 13 studies replicated, replication effect sizes average 75 percent originals. 8 replicate, mean relative effect size approximately zero. Bayesian mixture model estimated true-positive rate 67 percent, relative effect size true positives 71 percent. Even true-positive findings systematically overestimated original literature. Expert prediction markets closely tracked actual replication outcomes (mean market prediction 63.4 percent vs. actual replication rate 61.9 percent; Spearman correlation 0.842, p < 0.001), indicating domain experts can identify substantial accuracy studies hold . Camerer et al. (2018) conclude ‘systematic biases can reduced implementing pre-registration analysis plans reduce likelihood false positives registration reporting study results reduce effects publication bias inflating effect sizes’ (p. 642). SCORE confirms pattern larger scale: roughly half 164 replicated studies achieved statistical significance, effect sizes diminished median reductions exceeding 50 percent effect magnitude 80 percent explained variance.

what: 2.4 The Limits of Artificial Intelligence Prediction
title: Reproducibility Infrastructure and the Replication Crisis: Positioning zzcollab in Light of the SCORE Project
text: DARPA’s original motivation SCORE develop automated ‘credit score’ scientific claims. SCORE machine learning models detect signal far deployable standalone assessments. Expert peer judgment achieves approximately 75 percent accuracy predicting replication outcomes (Camerer et al. 2018). AI prediction problem primarily algorithmic. Current models lack access structured, machine-readable provenance information – environment specifications, analysis histories, data lineage records – needed make reliable predictions. Generating provenance data prerequisite, byproduct, solving prediction problem.
